title: Calculating deadweight and yield from a home pig
subtitle: What liveweight becomes after slaughter, and how to estimate your freezer fill.
excerpt: A pig that weighs 90 kg liveweight does not produce 90 kg of meat. Deadweight after slaughter is typically 72-76% of liveweight. Usable pork and pork products from the carcass are typically 70-80% of deadweight. Knowing these ratios before the pigs go to slaughter helps you plan freezer space, choose an abattoir slot timing, and set realistic expectations.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, Pig Carcase Classification guidance (ahdb.org.uk). Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig processing chapters. Home Food Safety guidance, Food Standards Agency.

glossaryTerms.definition: The weight of a live pig. The most straightforward way to estimate liveweight without scales is the tape measure method: multiply the heart girth squared (in cm) by the body length (in cm) and divide by 14,000 to give weight in kg.

The weight of the carcass after slaughter and dressing: head-on or head-off (abattoir convention varies), internal organs removed, skin intact or de-haired. Expressed as a percentage of liveweight, typically 72-76% for commercial pigs.

The ratio of deadweight to liveweight, expressed as a percentage. A pig with a dressing percentage of 74% means that for every 100 kg liveweight, 74 kg of carcass is returned. Fat cover, gut fill, and breed all influence the figure.

content.text: Before booking an abattoir slot, it helps to understand what
and then as finished product. For most smallholding pigs finished at 90-100 kg liveweight, the calculation is straightforward.

Use the tape-measure method: measure the heart girth (circumference just behind the front legs) in centimetres and the body length (from poll to tail base) in centimetres. Square the heart girth, multiply by the body length, and divide by 14,000. The result is liveweight in kilograms. The formula is approximate but accurate to within 10% for most breeds at finishing weight.

for a home-finished pig is typically 72-76%. For a pig at 90 kg liveweight, expect a carcass of 65-68 kg. Heavily fatted pigs dress higher; lean modern breeds dress lower. The abattoir will return the carcass hanging weight, which is the deadweight you pay the butcher to process.

Of the 65-68 kg carcass, roughly 70-80% becomes packaged pork, joints, mince, chops, sausages, bacon, and offal. The remainder is bone, skin, and trim. From a 90 kg pig you can realistically expect 45-54 kg of finished product. A chest freezer of 150-200 litres holds this comfortably with space to spare. Two weaners finished together fill two medium chest freezers.

Pigs slaughtered at a licensed abattoir for personal use fall within the rules for home consumption. The meat cannot be sold without abattoir inspection. Sharing within your household and family needs no licence.
